would not be acceptable to the ma
jority of the property owners. The
roadway specifications called for
are the same as those in the Den
mis- plans. Also, the sidewalks are
the same.


'Mr. Dennis further says that the
Spokane & Inland railroad would
not accept a roadway less than 100
feet wide. This railroad goes un-
der the trestle. No mention of the
desires of the railroad or what
would be acceptable to it are made
in the advertisement for bids ex-
cept that provision must be made
to keep the water from flowing into
the viaduct, and room must be pro-
vided for four tracks.


Mr. Dennis says that since the
railroad is to pay share of the
cost it should have a voice in de-
Gliding the acceptance of the plans.
The Dennis proposition did not
stipulate that the city could collect
cent from the company, because
Mr.. Dennis reserved that right unto
himself.


HE also says that he will send
the board letter protesting
against any acceptance of a bid
which does not include the same
plans and specifications as are on
file in the city engineers office. The
only plans on file there are the Den
mis- plans. Under this arrangement
it would be making Mr.. Dennis a
present of the job on his own plans,
as other contractors would prob
ably submit different plans.


Regarding the reference to the
charter of the city Mr.. Dennis is
using the words and not it's mean
ing to secure the adoption of his
own plans and not those of the
city engineer"
